talk_title: CODA: 3D Mapping of Tumors at Single‑Cell Resolution Across Organs and Species
MOA_summary: CODA reconstructs large tissue volumes (multi‑centimeter cubes) at sub‑micron resolution by iteratively registering serial H&E sections, segmenting tissue components with a CNN, and deconvolving cellular morphology, enabling 3D mapping of individual cells, vessels, nerves, and precancerous lesions across organs and species.
key_findings: Reconstructed a 3‑centimeter‑cube of human pancreas at sub‑micron resolution, positioning ~1 billion cells with >97% segmentation accuracy.
Identified 100–500 microscopic precursor lesions per healthy pancreas sample; disease‑adjacent pancreas contained up to 1 000 lesions.
Laser‑capture sequencing of individual lesions revealed distinct point‑mutation profiles, demonstrating multifocality in precancerous lesions.
Mapped entire fallopian tubes from seven healthy women and found STIC‑like lesions in all, challenging the prevailing belief that such lesions are rare in normal tissue.
Demonstrated that blood vessels and nerves follow a scale‑free fractal geometry across species (human, mouse, rhesus macaque) and developmental stages.
Developed generative AI tools (InterpolAI) to interpolate missing histology sections, achieving Pearson correlation >0.99 with ground‑truth images.
open_questions: What is the smallest length scale at which CODA provides biologically meaningful information for different research questions?
Can 3D snapshots of T‑cell spatial organization be used to predict tumor clearance or response to immunotherapy?

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Clinical Genomics Workflows for Precision Oncology

clinical_data.population: all solid tumors and hematologic malignancies

MOA_summary: AI methods are integrated into the MSK‑IMPACT clinical genomics pipeline to improve variant detection (deep learning callers, error‑modeling), functional interpretation (deep neural networks, AlphaMisSense, SpliceAI), and clinical decision support (LLMs with retrieval‑augmented generation, AI‑driven trial matching), thereby increasing sensitivity, reducing turnaround time, and expanding actionable insights across cancer types.
readouts: LLM‑based knowledge‑base querying (OncoKB, Molecular Oncology Almanac)
key_findings: Sequenced >100,000 patients using the MSK‑IMPACT 500‑gene tumor‑normal panel, generating a dataset that enables AI‑driven analyses.
Deep learning variant callers (e.g., DeepVariant) achieve higher sensitivity in difficult genomic regions compared with traditional callers.
AI‑based MSI detection (myMSI) reduced indeterminate cases relative to MSI‑Sensor, improving diagnostic confidence.
AlphaMisSense and SpliceAI improve pathogenicity prediction for missense and splice variants by incorporating protein structure and splicing models.
A neural network trained on 40,000 MSK‑IMPACT profiles predicts cancer type with ~80% accuracy overall and >90% for high‑confidence predictions.
LLM‑augmented tumor board agents (GPT‑4‑based) doubled decision‑support accuracy compared with GPT‑4 alone in a prospective evaluation.
open_questions: How can AI models be validated and regulated to ensure safety and equity across diverse patient populations?
What are the best practices for integrating AI‑generated confidence intervals into clinician decision‑making?
